Quotation: I think I got it. I think the major factors are very similar for all programming languages. So, basically, you calculate risk, you calculate gains, you calculate the effort you have to put into it. The major triggers are safety; how can I say, the cut of external dependencies, I think that it doesn't matter if you are speaking about a Java or a Python program, you don't want to use unnecessary third-party libraries, which can be changed, which can be outdated, which can be not supported. That's a problem for Java, Python, or C++, also because, of course, the safety and the readability or the learnability of the codes are similar factors. I see the factors are the same, the thresholds are different, it's likely in safety is much more an issue in C++ than, let's say, in Java. And it might be that Python is even a higher issue than in C++ because Python is a more dynamic language with more problems coming out from runtime. And, for example, if you can use any new Python feature typings, if you can use typings as a new language feature to decrease the runtime problems to decrease the possibility of runtime box, that you will press it more than in C++ or in Java to introduce such features. So, I see that the major factors might be the same for every language. And the threshold is different. In C++, it might be that the performance is more important than in Java. Java is that slow anyway, it doesn't matter how you improve it, it's not so much gain. Actually, if you have a web application, a database application, you don't mind the performance at all. Not that much. Okay?
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate | Improve code security | Improve code understanding | Minimizing dependency on third-party libraries
Concepts: Code Readability and Expressiveness | Decision-Making and Strategic Evaluation | Dependency and Architectural Simplification | Reliability and Correctness
SubCategories: Code Quality Improvements | Drivers for Source Code Rejuvenation | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: Sure. I will give a recent example of one of the changes we had with Kotlin 1.4. They have also released the stable version of the serialization library. For those who work in the JVM world a lot, if you want to work with objects in serialized JSON format, with the web world, there are well-established libraries that are third-party libraries, such as Jackson or JSON from Google. Recently, the Kotlin maintainers group released an embedded language version, so I think it was one of the things that we considered that would result in some different APIs for serialization. But we felt that the advantage of not relying on a third-party library and using a language feature, in this case, was worth it. So we decided to start using this kind of library for the serialization of our objects. Something that we also took into consideration was that I was following this library when it was in the unstable under development API. I enjoyed it very much. I had even done some tests, I was following it, and I was receiving feedback. At the moment version 1.0 stable was released, I thought: excellent, so this already had a community fire test, this is something that is here to stay. It will not be a project left in a corner. In that case, it was an analysis that resulted in a decision to try to use, in this serialization part (inaudible) [00:13:15] of an object. We have used this serialization library from Kotlin, so it is a pretty fresh example since this was a few months ago.
Codes: Minimizing dependency on third-party libraries | SCR requires careful planning
Concepts: Dependency and Architectural Simplification | Execution Strategy and Effort Management
SubCategories: Drivers for Source Code Rejuvenation | Execution and Operational Strategies

Quotation: The other trigger I also want to mention in C++ 11, is that before C++ 11, there was no standard multi-threading solution for C++. That doesn't mean that it wasn't multi-threading programming in C++, but you had to use third-party libraries, or you had to use non-standard features which might be platform-dependent. Replacing these features only gives you long-term guarantees that your code won't be corrupted. Either because the third-party library was outdated or just not supported anymore, or you don't need to bother with porting the features for new platforms. So, this is typically multi-setting. Not my project, but currently, I'm working on a project in which one of the major issues is renewing or refactoring the code, or modernizing the C++ code, which used exactly those third-party libraries. Again, before C++ 11 commercial libraries were being used instead of the standard parts, which were just not there. You know, C++ 11 also introduced several standard libraries, like regular expression libraries. Before that, there were no regular expression libraries, you had to go for third-party libraries. And there were, again, the maintenance issues, the no compatibility issues. It was 10 years ago, and that project, only now, decided to replace these libraries. You see that it wasn't something urgent, but sooner or later, they decided ”Okay, that's enough” it took too much work to maintain these third-party libraries.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate | Avoiding system obsolescence | Improve concurrency/asyncronous programming | Minimizing dependency on third-party libraries | SCR requires careful planning

Quotation:
W: Thanks. Okay. One moment. Do you remember any new language (construct) [00:23:35] you have adopted in your systems? For example, in the most recent version of C++, like C++, 11, and C++ 17.

P: Oh, well, lots of them. Again, we rewrite our code for using the standard threading features, which is C++ 11. Earlier, we used our third-party libraries, we rewrite the code with smart pointers, which, again, starting from C++ 11, also coming from C++ 14. We started to use lambda functions in C++. I think that lambda is coming from other languages Java, C#, everywhere else from C++ 11. Of course, it was a very important step of C++ 17 when the standard library appeared, so we have to communicate with the file system. Earlier, we used the boost libraries, which says in the third-party libraries.

P: That's a very famous library, and it’s hundreds of important libraries, but it's not an official part of the C++, but many of the libraries (appears here) [00:25:26]. So, we use the boost file system, and after C++ 17, we changed to the standard file system library. Yeah, I think these are the major features and also some smaller language features. Before C++ 11, you had to write every declaration in a very detailed way. From C++ 11, there is the auto keyword, which is very similar to Java’s var keyword or Scala’s var keyword. So, you know, you don't have to write the type of the variable, it just takes from the initialization automatically. That's a feature we've started to add more and more.
Codes: Minimizing dependency on third-party libraries

Quotation: Yeah. So, I think that if I want to summarize it, the most important thing for us was to decrease the outer dependencies. There are programming languages, like in Java, or other languages, where there are very good package managers, or even that are automatic stuff like in Maven, you know, if you use your project, then, even the build system can automatically download the dependencies, and all this kind of stuff. Unfortunately, C++ is very disadvantaged. In here, we see the use of all kinds of make systems, or the CMake library stuff. So, third-party dependencies are always problematic. Now, if you can replace third party dependencies, like third party libraries with standard stuff, which is given for you by the standard compiler, as a huge advantage for the continuous integration point of view, and the program management point of view, where you distribute your program, you don't need to distribute the third-party libraries, there are licensing problems sometimes, there are just distribution problems, you might not have root privileges on the system, you want to install your stuff. So, how you install third-party libraries, you know, and lots of similar issues. So, I think the major issue was to get rid of third-party dependencies, and the other stuff is to make a safer code, as a second most important point for us.
Codes: Improve code security | Minimizing dependency on third-party libraries
Concepts: Dependency and Architectural Simplification | Reliability and Correctness
SubCategories: Code Quality Improvements | Drivers for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation: I know that in some cases, for example, I need to use a component, a library, something like that from the company I work for, and this library is only compatible with a more recent version of Java. And then, for some reason, I need this library and I end up having to update the Java version.

Quotation: It depends on the project mainly. Most of the time, a major Qt upgrade (Qt4=>Qt5, Qt5=>Qt6) will require a more modern C++, thus allowing the full feature set. But there are some projects that will simply use these features when they consider most distributions will have a modern compiler handling them.

Quotation: Nowdays the modernization effor on c++ go a bit hand in hand with another effort as we are in the middle of the transition between Qt5 and Qt6, which will also bring a major, source and binary incompatible major release of the KDE frameworks, that leads to a general modernization effort.

Quotation:
Several inflection points observed in our adoption curves temporally align with major ecosystem-level events in the Python community. For example, Python 2 officially reached its end-of-life on January 1, 2020, after which it no longer received security patches or official support, effectively removing Python 2 as a viable long-term platform and 

Walter Mendonça et al.: Preprint submitted to Elsevier Page 12 of 19

Leveraging social media news 

increasing pressure for migration to Python 3.3 This transition was reinforced by key ecosystem decisions, including the release of Django 2.0 in December 2017, which dropped support for Python 2 entirely and required Python 3, as well as the coordinated phase-out of Python 2.7 support by major scientific libraries such as NumPy, aligned with the Python core team’s deprecation timeline.45 In parallel, the maturation of the Python tooling ecosystem between 2016 and 2019 — particularly the emergence and consolidation of static type checking tools such as mypy, which builds on function annotations — lowered the practical cost of adopting modern language features.6 While these events do not establish causality, their temporal proximity to the observed acceleration in feature adoption suggests that external, ecosystem-driven pressures played an important contextual role in reducing long-standing inertia and enabling broader source code rejuvenation.
Documents: 34-SCP - “It Makes the Code Clearer”: Why Developers Adopt Modern Python Features in Open Source Projects
Codes: Framework/libraries dependencies motivate SCR efforts | language version support discontinuity
Concepts: Dependency and Framework Pressure | Language Evolution Enforcement

Quotation: You can track it, but it all depends on the project. It all depends on the project's maturity. It depends on who provides maintenance since there is a cost to keep up with it. As I mentioned, I work on a project that is still on Python 2. It is a project that comes from 2010, and there are few people in my work maintaining it. We are aware that it is risky to use a discontinued language version, but due to the effort it would take to update the dependencies to a newer version using Python 3, we decided to prioritize other things. It is a trade-off situation. It depends a lot on the context of the project you are working on. This project that I am working on in Kotlin, for example, is a much more recent project. It is a project that started early last year, so it is a little over 12 months old. For us, having the flexibility to change that, is less cumbersome. We can do that. I would say that the leaps in technology are not that great.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate | Project complexity might hinder SCR efforts | language version support discontinuity
Concepts: Decision-Making and Strategic Evaluation | Effort, Cost & Practical Constraints | Language Evolution Enforcement
SubCategories: Challenges & Impediments | Drivers for Source Code Rejuvenation | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: First, to be aligned to the language’s platform itself, to not make the code [degrated], to not become outdated to the point there is no longer compatibility between versions. Did you notice, for example, that Google no longer allows the hosting of apps with outdated libraries on its Play Store platform? It means that if you make an app, you will always need to keep maintenance on the libraries that it references, update them constantly, otherwise you are banned from Google's application hosting platform. This is a classic example that code outdating implies the unfavorable issue of using the software itself, the language, and so on.
Codes: Avoiding system obsolescence

Quotation: In the case of the linux client here at the company, I have been doing it weekly, because like, here I have been using GTK instead of Qt, but it is the same. Because GTK has evolved, the language has evolved and I want the client to be more readable, I do it weekly. In the end, if you don't do this, nobody will be interested anymore, the software will get old and will die....so this work has to be done periodically or it will die.
Codes: Avoiding system obsolescence | Improve code understanding | Incremental SCR effort
Concepts: Code Readability and Expressiveness | Execution Strategy and Effort Management | Preventing System Obsolescence
SubCategories: Code Quality Improvements | Drivers for Source Code Rejuvenation | Execution and Operational Strategies

Quotation: I don't know if I understand correctly: for performance improvements, I think that a lot of languages, like Java or C++, (are explaining) in the change nodes: if you use that feature, you will gain some memory consumption or you’ll have a smaller code or something like that. And, as I read them, I try to remember that. If I’m writing a code, I try to include this new feature, such as a move constructor for my class, and, as for the compiler, we use (coverity scan). And this will improve the performance. For example, often, we don’t measure if that one exact change improves the performance or not. For big projects, we have some regression tests as well as performance tests. So, before we release, we trigger a performance test that performs several analyses on a lot of tools. And we often study the performance project or projects. We do not measure every small change before the release. And, if we have performance issues, we try to investigate what was the wrong decision we made in the code.
Codes: Relevance of regression/automated testing
Concepts: Availability of Automated and Regression Testing

Quotation: Normally, you just change the code as it is, you just change compilers, go on to the next one and then you can say that you’re compiling with the new version. But you didn’t change your code, as language evolution has a huge concern with retrocompatibility. C++ does not break retroactively, it’s very rare, so you can change the version and compiler somewhat safely. You’d still need to perform tests; As I said, people have performance issues, but normally, you’ll be able to change. What could be a pain is the improper use of language, which is normally already incorrect while you were using it.
Codes: Relevance of regression/automated testing
Concepts: Availability of Automated and Regression Testing

Quotation: There is no major direction given. Each project does as it wants. For instance, calligra chose for a long time to keep compatibility with some very old libraries because one of our known users, Jolla, is still using Qt 5.6, prohibiting some new constructions from being used.
Codes: Backward compatibility constraints hindering SCR | Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts
Concepts: Compatibility & Legacy Constraints | External Dependencies & Environment Constraints

Quotation: how will `new Promise` work? ES6 Promises are not in all browsers and dash.js does not have a Promise polyfill dependency right now.

Quotation: Therefore, the change is not simple, as to perform it you need to change the compiler, because new language features are not available in the one you have. I don’t recall exactly what version our compiler was, but it’s certainly not from this year. Our compiler is already old. But then binary compatibility issues arise. You’ll only be able to modify your version when the upstream chain changes. That way, until it reaches you, everyone, every library in use will need to change, and then you can change yours too.
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts | Lack of retrocompatibility might hinder SCR efforts
Concepts: Compatibility & Legacy Constraints | External Dependencies & Environment Constraints

Quotation: When frameworks have drastic updates, they provide a migrating tool, there are also migrating guides, but preferably, an automated migration tool helps a lot. It’s a challenge if there are retrocompatibility issues, especially if they’re not algorithmically soluble.
Codes: Lack of retrocompatibility might hinder SCR efforts

Quotation: Whichever is breaking change. If a language has no concern in making a good amount compatible with it when it changes, you’ll have to open two APIs and then go on mapping them. Nowadays, I see that the languages have great concern in keeping the compatibility. With TypeScript , it was even funny because it does not follow the semantic version, so from version 3 to 4, which we updated recently, it kept retrocompatibility, and it wasn’t any trouble. Python was quite troublesome to update from version 2 to 3, we had to change many libraries. Dart’s null safety part is being done little by little on flutter, they’re even releasing command line tools. I often see that the languages are concerned with simplifying the migration process, precisely so people can follow the evolution and avoid the situation that happens a lot with these older languages. Like Java, where developers got stuck in an older version and wouldn’t update because there was too much code in it, and end up having to stay in an older version for a longer time. The challenge is to see what crashes. But nowadays, it’s much easier than it used to be half a decade ago. I started programming ten years ago, when changing versions was much more traumatic, nowadays it’s much smoother.
Codes: Lack of retrocompatibility might hinder SCR efforts

Quotation:
Yeah I am very new to ES6 modules and I was in a hurry so the format I used was mostly determined by a search-and-replace. Also I had to be careful to only use ES5 

features _outside_ the modules because I found that using `babel` to transpile from ES6 to ES5 added a huuuge amount of overhead even if I was only using one or two features in one or two places.

Quotation: I guess there’s a personal side to it, as today we’d rather be more stable. Some people use Java 6, Java 7 even today. Because they already have a very big code base that’s troublesome to migrate. But personally, I’d rather always be up to date, because the longer you wait to migrate, the worse this change becomes. For example, in the case of this framework change to Vue’s version 3, if we waited years and years before migrating, it would be a lot of work. But I could test, migrate it little by little as it launches. You see, version 3 is closer to version 2 than it is to version 1. Then, version 4 will launch afterward, and migrating from 2 to 4 is much more troublesome. We have this application in Python 2.7, that we won’t even migrate, as it’s too obsolete. We’re making a new one to replace it, this time in Kotlin, it’s not in Python anymore. We’re being more careful: When a new Kotlin version launches, we update immediately, because if you go, “I don’t have time now”, you’ll never have time, and the longer it takes, the worse it gets. Especially in those newer languages, that are coming with components like null safety, better typing and IDE integration, it spares a lot of trouble. The change of what we had in JavaScript to TypeScript is not really language evolving in itself. TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript. It’s 100% compatible with JavaScript, it just adds typing. In our team, we consider typing to be a very good thing to avoid bugs, so what we’re doing is migration: to migrate everything that’s JavaScript into TypeScript. It’s the same notion, it’s like a new language feature, that’s typing, but you end up needing to change your code, as it will not take the non typed part. We do it little by little, the sooner the better.

Quotation:
K: Yeah. Participant, thank you very, very much for this interview. It is essential to us, it is very, very important, I'm sure. We understand much better now, we understand, we have a better understanding of these language scenarios, after the answers you've given us. Please feel free to talk as much as you like, because I'm sure it's a very interesting topic.

P: Just a few interesting things you might be interested in. One of my peers is working on language evolution stuff, because we were aware, and we have detected several side effects of the new C++ version. Let's imagine you have a C++ code that compiles (and notifies) [00:57:51], you get warnings of any side effects, it's absolutely valid C++ code in C++ 89 or 98 version, it's a standard C++. When it comes to the new version, the new C++ 11 version, the very same code is compiled, and it's again, without warnings, and the program behaves completely differently. That happens. I can show you... I don't know whether you are familiar with C++. Do you know C++?

K: I know it, but I don’t use it. I actually utilize Fortran, almost daily, but I do know it. I’ve learned the fundamentals, very basic fundamentals of C++. So, I have a very rough knowledge.

P: We can write 20 line programs with very fundamental, very trivial features, like using a vector, and put elements on the vector, and one program writes 11111 the other program we will write 12345. The very same syntax, the very same code, you just switch on a different compiler flag. You say, compile me with C++ original, or compile maybe C++ 11 version. You might see that that's a very special example perhaps, but it's happened solidly in every 10,000 lines once, but if you have, let's say, 10 million lines of code, then you are afraid of that. There are still 1000 possibilities. My Ph.D. student started to work on whether we can automatically detect this kind of different behavior. The idea is that you can parse the abstract syntax tree with the old compiler settings and the new compiler settings, and you can compare whether they are similar, or whether they are identical or not. The problem is, of course, that there are a lot of changes, which are not (changing) [00:01:29] the semantics, just different naming inside the abstract syntax. It's very hard to make such a tool work automatically. But it was an attempt for us, to them, to make such a thing.
Codes: Language evolution introducing silent behavior changes | Language evolution might introduce breaking changes | Language evolution triggering hidden regressions | Project complexity might hinder SCR efforts
Concepts: Effort, Cost & Practical Constraints | Language Evolution Complexity & Instability

Quotation: Yes. It’s like i’ve told you last time, unless your project is a toy. If your project is a toy, there’s no problem, you can take a lot of risks. If your project is in use though, like in my case, I work on a spot in the company where we are like a middle ground that’s used by a lot of people; (it just can’t crash) [00:33:52]. It can’t. It can’t stop working, you can’t risk this kind of thing. (But) [00:34:01], tools that help are always welcome. Now, to be honest, tools have improved incredibly in recent years. In the last 10 years, (CLion) [00:34:17], Visual Studio are tools that help a lot. The (Clang) [00:34:23] man, the (Clang) [00:34:23] came with a… these people have a great tool base, really great - code analysis, formatting; they understand the language, they make the compiler, you see? So like, it’s a really great group.
Codes: Project complexity might hinder SCR efforts

Quotation: And for the people who use Java, as there are various, small systems, the susceptibility for evolving is higher. But only because the susceptibility is higher, that doesn’t mean that evolving it is a small job, as there’s this whole thing of getting stuck in the framework. But I believe it has to do with newer and older languages.
Codes: Programming language flexibility | SCR is a non-trivial effort

Quotation: It's not always possible to update, first of all, because you work with some mixed environments. This means that, in the development area, you have validation, development and production environments. And it is not always easy for you to tell every user of these environments, using a certain technology/language, that they will have to stop for a day, or a week, so the system can undergo an update. Or for us to refactor the system according to newer versions of languages, components, library, APIs, etc.
Codes: SCR is a non-trivial effort | environment configurations might hinder SCR efforts
Concepts: Effort, Cost & Practical Constraints | External Dependencies & Environment Constraints

Quotation:
Thinking a lot about the front end, which I have more experience with, normally when a new version comes out, we have a lot of third-party libraries that we use in our project. And then we have to be very careful to manage which libraries support this new version and which don't. So, many times, at least the last time I did it, it was a very manual job to have to try and make updates.

For example, I'm in version 48 and I want to go to 52. I made the update to 49, I saw if everything was working, I went to 50, I saw if it was working. So, it's a very manual job.

That's why I go into it. If it's a very expensive job, for a version that's not even a market standard, the features may not even be that relevant, I think it makes sense to insure it. But for me, the biggest challenge is certainly compatibility and how you can guarantee the compatibility of the new version with your services.

Quotation: And, of course, in my opinion, sometimes companies are mad about it: why would I pay you a month’s salary for doing practically nothing other than enhancing your code or something like that. The company cannot resell it just because it's written in the newer language, or something like that. Of course, there are also times when you cannot move on. For example, with this JavaScript project, we couldn't move on for a long time since we had dependencies that are deprecated or that are now legacy. And there weren’t any newer versions that we could use. So that's another reason I experienced that might also prevent the code or the language evolution.
Codes: Companies may not consider SCR efforts Relevant | Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts
Concepts: External Dependencies & Environment Constraints | Organizational & Managerial Constraints

Quotation: Existing resources have better integration with APIs from the same library I think that's because those features have been provided by the Qt framework long before they were standardized in C++ (e.g. QThread for std::thread, QFuture for std::future, QtConcurrent is sort of an equivalent to std::async) and they generally integrate better with the rest of the Qt-based codebase than their modern C++ counterparts.
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts

Quotation: For futures and async, there is a Qt framework (QtConcurrent) which provides similar APIs that are easier to integrate into existing Qt projects
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts

Quotation: For instance, std::thread. It's a welcome addition to the C++ standard library, and it was badly needed. But the need for an abstraction to system threading APIs is not new, and for a long time Qt had a QThread class, deeply rooted in its object and event models. If you are writing a Qt application, std::thread is more cumbersome to use than QThread.
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts

Quotation: For me, most of the time, since I don't really have the time to investigate the new language features, or the new library features, the reimplemented stuff, and so on. So, it was quite challenging to study all these things and then adapting the things I’ve learned to my particular system or systems. However, I’ve finished my Ph.D. studies, so maybe I will have the time to deep dive into Python 3.10, for example. And, of course, another main challenge for me was that, if you have some dependencies that are deprecated or that are legacy now, then you have quite a hard time to evolve your system or update your language or the language you use. For example, with NNG, we switched to the ESM module loader and there were tons of deprecated dependencies. So, we had to find, for each deprecated dependency, a new one or an updated one or a four which worked as well as the original one, but also with the ESM system loader. This is also quite a hard task. Another example: in C++, you had the Boost Library. And you work with that exact boost version. But if you update your language, you have to find another boost, which has also been evolved. So, your functions are getting renamed and so on. So, you have to understand what happened since then in a dependency, like in boost for C++. And yeah, this is the case as well for Java. And for Python too.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate | Difficulty in following language evolution | Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts | SCR requires careful planning
Concepts: Decision-Making and Strategic Evaluation | Execution Strategy and Effort Management | External Dependencies & Environment Constraints | Language Evolution Complexity & Instability
SubCategories: Challenges & Impediments | Execution and Operational Strategies | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: Framework libraries influence rejuvenation Since the Qt API is mostly frozen in C++98/C++03 era (and C++11 support in Qt can mostly be reduced just to auto and lambdas), there's little incentive for the community to actively use latest C++ features.
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts

Quotation: I don't know. Maybe because KDE uses Qt API for threads and asynchronous stuff, so a lot of signal/slots, etc... And also it's more advanced stuff, so naturally used less.
Codes: Delayed adoption of new versions | Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts
Concepts: Adoption Strategy and Timing | External Dependencies & Environment Constraints
SubCategories: Challenges & Impediments | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: I think the reason for this is the very good support for concurrent programming in Qt ...This is available in Qt already for very long time and many projects, including LabPlot and Cantor, are using this. Right now there is no need and no plans on our side to move away from what Qt is offering. This can change in future, of course, but right now our spare time is better invested in other areas.
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts

Quotation: I would, but we always have to keep in mind that to evolve the codebase for language versions, simply updating our codebase is not enough. In any project, nowadays, you use third-party dependencies that, many times, are not going to be updated by these third parties. I would use it, but first, I would have to check if the third-party libraries I am using are updated. An example of this (not of using a tool, because I have never used one) but an example of the above case: I don't work so much with front-end, but I am aware that components of our code cause problems when upgrading the version of the framework, Vue.js, from version 2 to version 3. Many things in our code are easy to migrate, but there are libraries that we use that are not migrated, so we end up barely migrating. We can migrate our code, but we will break all binds with our libraries. The answer to your question is: it depends. It depends on the condition in which the language and the libraries are.
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts

Quotation: In this case, it would be the other dependents being used alongside the language. If you use only Java, there is not much challenge in evolving your code. But, for example, if you use a Lombok project that doesn’t get updated for Java 10, and you’re on Java 9, it stops working. So, it’s more of an issue with the dependencies that you use with your project than with the language itself. As in many cases, at least from my experiences, they don’t go 100% aligned with the language’s version. For example, Java 10 is launched, and the dependency itself, some plugin you have, has already been launched for Java 10 too. Sometimes, it takes a week or two, or even much more time than that. So, I’d say the main challenge would be the dependencies that you use.
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts | Language evolution might introduce breaking changes
Concepts: External Dependencies & Environment Constraints | Language Evolution Complexity & Instability

Quotation:
R: For example, we have the limitation of the framework. You can use the framework 2.1. Then there’s a feature that’s new for all 3, but you can’t use it.

W: I see, so you need to wait until the compiler gets updated so you can….

R: It’s not even about updating the compiler, it’s about updating the framework that’s currently in use, all applications, so that you can apply new features.

W: So, the framework can also become a bottleneck and make evolution harder?

R: The framework can become a bottleneck, as well as the compiler. Sometimes, you have a legacy code, a framework compiler and the changes that you bring into the application of are significant. So, in order to change from a version into another, you need to abandon the legacy.
Codes: Framework/library dependencies hinder SCR efforts

For example, if you have any project with Java 8 and migrate to Java 9, you need to create – if I’m not mistaken – a file to inform which modules will form this new project you’re creating. So, there’s a little detail: could you use a single version to do everything? Yes and no. It depends a lot on what you’re using to complement the job.

Quotation: Four. in C++ 11, it was a huge step forward. It was a really large part, I have a lecture when I have a showcase, which shows that... I don't remember the correct numbers, but the size of the C++ standard originally was something like 800 pages. The C++ 11 standard was 1200, and currently, it's about 1600. So, it has doubled, which means that, partially, the language features (it says) [00:53:58] partially the libraries are doubled. So, the evolution is extremely fast, and to follow this, not only on the theoretical level, but on the level which I expect my program to be. So to apply these changes in practice, but at the same time still, be aware of the new traps and pitfalls. That's hard because it means you have to apply them, you have to try them out. You don't just read the books or the blogs, you have to apply them to existing problems and see the side effects. The problem is, if you have a new language feature, that's okay, everybody understands, if you have another new language feature, everybody understands that, but if you have, let's say, these two new language features, they sometimes interact. So, the complexity is not additive, but rather multiplicative, and the growth of the complexity. Imagine you have 100 new language features and all interact with it, or the others, you know, and that's where the complexity is, that even in the best effort, these language features always interact with each other, and that can have side effects, even such side effects, the standardization committee never thought. And there are debates, of course in the standardization committee under the conferences about us. So yeah, it's very hard, even for me, so I can imagine that those programmers who have daily work just to produce the code, and have fewer opportunities to do this. That's not easy.
Codes: Difficulty in following language evolution | Feature interactions create unexpected side effects
Concepts: Language Evolution Complexity & Instability | Technical Debt & System Quality Constraints

Quotation: Well, today I struggle to follow it, as there are many versions, and you need to write code for it, perform tests, do a lot of stuff, so nowadays we don’t really have time to keep up with this technical part of language evolution. At least where I worked, few teams engaged in this, specifically. There’s a lot to do instead of assigning someone to look out for code evolving opportunities, it’s not common. So that’s why I have a hard time doing this. You get a version now, then six months to one year later, there’s a new one already. Keeping up with evolution is really hard. When the versions were released on a 4, 5-year basis, it was better. But now, that it’s yearly, or even twice a year, it’s just so hard. But there are also languages that don’t change per se, what really changes is the compiler. When it’s just the compiler, or interpreter, we can follow more easily, but when it’s the language itself, it’s harder to follow.
Codes: Difficulty in following language evolution

Quotation: You’ll need to pressure the upstream people so you can change too, you see? In case you’re working like this. So like, there’s the compiler change, and depending on how you use the language, in C++, you can incur your code in .... And then a compiler change can cause an incorrect execution, as you were counting on some behavior that’s not defined, or it can cause a performance loss. I’ve seen it happen in the last company that I worked for; we were working with version 14 and we tried to change into 17. When they started making performance tests, there was an (impact) on the performance. Until I left them, the cause remained unidentified. Normally, this is what happens: when you change compilers, the performance gets better. You see? The compiler people are not dumb, they also follow this way of thinking: if you’re making a change, it has to upgrade the security, performance, or usage. And they are very careful with C++ performance. I don’t know what happened, why the performance got worse, but it was detected that it did. And my guess is that someone was using a behavior anti error software in the code and it got a false performance. And maybe in our specific case it wouldn’t have caused any issues, but with the compiler change, the compiler people corrected the problem and it caused an impact. But I don’t know, we didn’t get to investigate it and I left the company before we solved this problem. But there was also another situation. We were on compiler, if I’m not mistaken, 5 or 4 point something, and we were going to compiler 10 - we were going to skip 5 versions. It’s risky, and there was no test coverage, increasing the risk even more.
Codes: Upgrading language implementation (compiler) might compromise performance

The project maintainers decide on their own what they need and when. I'm mainly working on LabPlot and Cantor and we have c++11 as the minimum supported version now.

Quotation: In my experience, and the details are in the paper - you might have got it, or I can send it to you if you don't have it -, for example, we worked with NNG, which, in Europe, was, a leading industrial company which mainly worked on the GPS mostly for cars. Back then, I think they had the Android application, although I think that they deleted it already. They are now just reselling their system to car factories. They’ve had some big issues moving to C++ 11 because, in the car industry, it's not enough to have a new feature and, hooray, we are changing to that. They have several constraints and laws. They often have some formally validated or verified language compiler, which was C++ 2003 back then. So, they had issues changing to C++ 11 as several car companies told them that they cannot purchase or order code that has C++ 11 features on it because their restrictions made it impossible. So, the industrial restrictions and rules are one thing that might prevent the developer from evolving a system or changing to a newer version of a language. And, of course, sometimes people are lazy and they just don't want to modify an already existing codebase that works well and which is tested, and the tests are passing and everything's fine. So why should you modify that? And, of course, in my opinion, sometimes companies are mad about it: why would I pay you a month’s salary for doing practically nothing other than enhancing your code or something like that. The company cannot resell it just because it's written in the newer language, or something like that. Of course, there are also times when you cannot move on. For example, with this JavaScript project, we couldn't move on for a long time since we had dependencies that are deprecated or that are now legacy. And there weren’t any newer versions that we could use. So that's another reason I experienced that might also prevent the code or the language evolution.
Codes: Company Restrictions may hinder SCR efforts | Keep the systems running and evolutive maintenances are more important than SCR efforts

Quotation: Qt in the past had library level features for all of those things as were missing from the base language, so we use a lot of those features from Qt (QThreads, QFutures or a slightly different approach in KDE frameworks as KJob) we will eventually slowly move to standard c++ versions of those, but will take time, as porting things that are working is not the immediate priority at the moment. for instance it's not long we started to use more of the stl standard containers as std:vector, typle and so on rather than QVector, QList and so on.
Codes: Incremental SCR effort | Keep the systems running and evolutive maintenances are more important than SCR efforts
Concepts: Execution Strategy and Effort Management | Organizational & Managerial Constraints

Quotation: This is a discussion that has no immediate answer. At Foton, I update the J2EE version, which will use newer versions, newer libraries, different frameworks. In this case, you have to redevelop. Sometimes you would not even need to redevelop some old code, but sometimes it is worth it. There are situations that we do. Now, when you have old libraries, cryptographic libraries that a client implemented, you use it the way it is, if it is working. When it's a big change, you will develop a new, more radical version of the product, and you will recompile, at least, to a more recent version. If not, you will redevelop a lot, but at least you will compile. Some things will be redeveloped, many others will be recompiled with newer versions. In the majority of cases, you upgrade when you need a library update. In general, you will not refactor an old code just because you changed the language to put a different interaction. The code is already tested and validated in production. It is not justifiable to take the risk, to increase cost and the risk of crashing in production for things that are already validated and structured. At most, you will recompile with a newer version. Some maintenances are structural, those at the architecture level and some maintenances are peripheral, such as screen and functionalities that use the same structure. For example, at Foton, we work a lot with the banking system, with banking transactions. So, for example, I have the SAC, deposit, statement, balance, transfer, and bill payment authorizers. If you have changed the authorization framework, you have a base that serves all authorizations. You will need to test all of them. But as a developer, as an architect, you will make that change in the base, in the higher classes, in the libraries, and you will validate with one or another functionality. Then, you will pass that on to the developers to make other changes. Then, when the system goes for a more complete test, you will validate the rest, but you will not validate the whole system, because sometimes you have 100 transactions, 100 features that use the same base class. There is a framework to know where you are working and what is impacted. But the focus is on the change you made, and you will not do everything over again. Each case is unique and has characteristics that sometimes you have to adapt to different application servers. For example, I developed with JBoss, and now I have to do the same system running on WebSphere, or Weblogic. That often doesn't affect all the functionalities. It affects your infrastructure a lot. It is much more at the architectural level. There are patterns that functionalities use. So if you validate and it worked, you believe the rest will also work. Then, you move on to the test team. You will need to do the full suite yourself. There are many cases, just like everything else in life.
Codes: Keep the systems running and evolutive maintenances are more important than SCR efforts

Quotation: Well, there are two cases: that software we keep running without following the evolution, small changes in framework and language. For example, for an application made with Java 6, or even Java 8, we just keep it running, adding new features but keeping frameworks like spring, (init. 10:53). We keep the same versions and keep it running, so a future migration causes a bigger impact in this software. The other case is to evolve gradually, not in every version but in each maintenance, dealing with the technical debt during the developing process itself. In this case, we evolve the software gradually, but always keep the versions updated.
Codes: Incremental SCR effort | Keep the systems running and evolutive maintenances are more important than SCR efforts | Reducing technical debt
Concepts: Continuous Technical Debt Management | Execution Strategy and Effort Management | Organizational & Managerial Constraints

Quotation: Anyway, there have been many evolutions of the language. What happens? When you have a modularized code, the software is less affected by new updates. So you might need to tweak something, but it's usually not that much of a problem. Libraries are a big problem. With updates, you may need to redevelop a lot of things. You may have to update something because the new release changes the way you use that library. That is is very common, and the language itself has many things that have been modernized within it. So when working with a logger, you need to adapt some compatibility mechanisms because it has evolved and changed its structure. But the main problem usually is the release of library versions instead of the new versions of the programming language. The Lambda Expressions didn't exist, and old components still worked.
Codes: Language evolution is not that relevante to motivate maintenance efforts

Quotation: From Java 9 onward, the module system was introduced. This system is responsible for defining and organizing modules within an application. When you have an application running on Java 8 and, for some reason, you import a Maven dependency that brings a chain of transitive dependencies, if any element in that dependency tree was built targeting Java 9, it can cause problems when you deploy the application to IBM WebSphere.

some long type (especially with C++ template types it might be harder to read than just auto). range-for idea is not that different from Qt's Q_FOREACH which was already used a lot, so it makes sense to move to C++ standard version (and range-for generates slightly better machine code than Q_FOREACH). So, let's go. When we talk about language, there's no way to talk about framework if you use a framework, okay? So, always to talk about language and framework.

Quotation: To support new language features... Often issues of expressiveness and clarity. I will give you an example: we had to adapt a lot of our code for the application I am working on now, which is a server for management and condition of military operations - a lot of our development used to be called "stringified types" - this is very common in that code where you see that your entity identifier is a string, someone's name is a string, these are all "stringified" types. There are features of Kotlin that have come recently to encapsulate these primitive types. It has more semantics to it. Instead of having a string type representing a password, you can have a (caps) type [00:06:41], which is a password type. That came in Kotlin 1.4, and we evolved our code to adapt it, so that's one of the reasons. Another reason, which is also quite typical, which is a thing that is a bit newer and more smoothly evolving, is asynchronous and concurrency libraries. We use Kotlin Coroutines, which is one of the things that came with the first version of the language. We started using coroutine libraries in the experimental version, which was already stable, but still had to have one or two API adaptations. We started using it in the experimental version, so we adapted some of our code as it progressed from experimental to a stable version with the stable API. These are two examples that I can tell you about the evolution we make in the code, not to adapt to the language, but to try to keep up with the latest versions of the tools we use.
Codes: Improve code expressiveness | Improve code understanding | Improve concurrency/asyncronous programming
Concepts: Code Readability and Expressiveness | Performance and Efficiency

And this leads to many issues, such as parallelism issues. Concurrency control with pure C is really difficult. So there was an immediate need of converting legacy code to C++ in order to enable the use of the replace folders, the gauge folders and also use the frameworks (concurrency computing) alongside them, so you could work with a lot of simultaneous data, with no risk of compromising it.

There was definitely a decision to change to C17, and they did it, but you don’t do this every day. There are language innovations every day, you know? People say: there’s going to be this and that, and also the compiler extensions, but you won’t use it. When the version comes out, there’s this decision to change.
Codes: Delayed adoption of new versions | SCR requires careful planning
Concepts: Adoption Strategy and Timing | Execution Strategy and Effort Management

Quotation:
I know there are others that are not long-term, but just to give you an example of the context of what I want to talk about, I follow things daily, especially on LinkedIn, on YouTube, the things people do that are interesting, from libraries. However, I don't worry so much about the version number, you know? Sometimes the version already covers everything we need, we use it, because what I think is cool is that, sometimes we use a new version of a code, but even so we code in an old way, because usually the code is backward compatible, like the versions are backward compatible.

So the cool thing is not even, like, I use the new version, it doesn't necessarily mean that I'm coding in a more recent way, you know? So, what I try to evolve, which is usually the routine, is to see every day a post on LinkedIn or on YouTube of people commenting about the tools I use. Exactly, like, the versions I use already cover this type of code, but I still use an old way of doing it, the same method, the same function, or the same functionality.

Sometimes I see that instead of using one thing, I use another thing. So, to conclude, I don't know if you could understand it right, but yes, I follow it daily, and I try to put new things, new ways of doing it, with the same version, the code, instead of worrying too much about the version of the code, right?
Codes: Delayed adoption of new versions

Quotation:
I'm not a person who releases a new version and already wants to test it, thinking about a framework or a specific language. If a 3.2 version of Vue comes out, I'm not interested in doing a little project to test and see these things. I try to wait for the moment when it is more consolidated, it is a more stable version.

Then I usually try to create some parallel projects, very simple, to see my adaptation to the new version. And from there, liking the way I worked, the flow that was there, the development, I start to think about bringing it to the work system, I suggest doing a migration.
Codes: Delayed adoption of new versions

Quotation:
K: Yes, sure. What means do you utilize to acquire this knowledge about the new features?

P: Typically, we use three, four years delays. Currently, C++ 20 is one year old. We just started to try out C++ 20, so I don't expect that we will apply them in the next two years. Perhaps three or four years after the feature appeared, we started to use them. That was a long-term experience. Our C++ 11 features, we started using C++ 13, or 14, or something like that. It's a bit different, however, when we are speaking about language, not about language features, but let's say libraries, as I mentioned, that boost libraries, as third-party libraries are sometimes very similar to the standard library stuff, so, to change from boost file system to the standard file system didn't take three years. It was just in that same year because it was just not new knowledge. We know that “Okay, now, this has a standard version, it has almost the same behavior, almost the same syntax.”, so, you don't need to rewrite the code, you only need to change the references from the boost library to the standard.
Codes: Delayed adoption of new versions | Prior knowledge reduces adoption effort
Concepts: Adoption Strategy and Timing | Knowledge, Experience, and Team Dynamics
SubCategories: Human and Social Strategies | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: That depends. First of all, I think that it very much depends on the experience of the programmer. There are two different levels of knowledge of the programming language, you might read the blogs, you might read the conference lectures, you might have heard about the new features, you know it theoretically, but you never used it, you never experienced the world backwards. So, you don't know what the traps and pitfalls are, okay? In that level, the very first level, even if you knew those new features, even if you touch the code, you won’t use these new features, because you are not confident to use them. If you have tried that, and if it has worked, if you were happy, you had positive feedback, because there were fewer bugs, or it was easier to develop, then let's say you have started to be confident, on (that feature) [00:21:05] (in) [00:21:06] that case, of course, when you touch the code, you don't just reproduce the original, you try to modernize the code. It's coming in bursts. Now, still, there might be a blocking factor. Your program should be (cooperated) [00:21:29] with other modules. And it's always a question of whether these modifications can work together with another code, let's say if the interface between the modules is based on the old techniques, then you can't modify your code because there is somebody with who you have to communicate with and that (used other) [00:21:57] kind of interface, like in C++, you can use these mention smart pointers, but if you're cooler, (plus) [00:22:08] you want (old style) [00:22:09] pointers, then, okay, you might use smart pointers internally. But that's unlikely to work. So, it's, again, a factor that might block the rewriting of the code, but if you are lucky, then you are developed to design your program, like our program is a pluggable infrastructure, the plugins are typically independent enough so I can change the implementation wholly inside my plugins.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate | Delayed adoption of new versions | SCR requires careful planning
Concepts: Adoption Strategy and Timing | Decision-Making and Strategic Evaluation | Execution Strategy and Effort Management
SubCategories: Execution and Operational Strategies | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: Yes, there’s that too. When language evolves, you need a new compiler. Many times, compiler evolution doesn’t happen alongside language evolution. For example, we’re now in 2021; last year, the 2020 standard was dropped, and we started to work with the 2023 standard. The compiler people are in this committee, and the committee presents papers on language evolution. The compiler people often take part in this work, in order to evaluate the viability of these submissions. And then those things start to show up as new extensions, you can be on the 2020 standard with a 2023 extension. Normally, when working with a stable system, you’ll hardly be ahead, you normally stay behind. That’s what I’m talking about: Today, we’re on version 17, and I don’t know if we’ll be able to migrate into version 20. Right? What I feel is that we’ll just end up migrating to version 20 when 23 comes out. Why? Because of this stability issue, there’s a need to ensure the continuity of the service.
Codes: Delayed adoption of new versions

Quotation:
K: Right. Anyway, we have two more scale questions, then we’re free for you to discuss the important things you want to discuss with us. So, the next question is, on the same scale from one to five, one is never, three is half of the time and five is always, how often would you say you implement the changes in the updates that you learned about the language in your existing code?

P: Well, if we touch the code, then we almost always try to use the new features. So, let's say four, but, as I mentioned, we don't touch the code just to modernize it. What usually happens is that we have a trigger, as we spoke about, to modify the code, and if we modify, of course, we always try to apply the newest features. But that might not be general for every developer in the industry, because I have been working with my Ph.D. students, very advanced guys themselves, language developers too. They are very sensitive to these new features. I can imagine that an average programmer does not apply the new features. Our average programmer can be on the scale two, for our programmers, let’s say four.

K: I see. So, for this question, I want to ask: how hard is it to keep updated on the new language features? I understand that it might not be as hard for you, since you are very, very internal to the languages, and you have to teach, you go to conferences, but how hard, would you say, from one to five? From one to five, where one is very easy, and five, it's very hard, almost impossible.

P: Four. in C++ 11, it was a huge step forward. It was a really large part, I have a lecture when I have a showcase, which shows that... I don't remember the correct numbers, but the size of the C++ standard originally was something like 800 pages. The C++ 11 standard was 1200, and currently, it's about 1600. So, it has doubled, which means that, partially, the language features (it says) [00:53:58] partially the libraries are doubled. So, the evolution is extremely fast, and to follow this, not only on the theoretical level, but on the level which I expect my program to be. So to apply these changes in practice, but at the same time still, be aware of the new traps and pitfalls. That's hard because it means you have to apply them, you have to try them out. You don't just read the books or the blogs, you have to apply them to existing problems and see the side effects. The problem is, if you have a new language feature, that's okay, everybody understands, if you have another new language feature, everybody understands that, but if you have, let's say, these two new language features, they sometimes interact. So, the complexity is not additive, but rather multiplicative, and the growth of the complexity. Imagine you have 100 new language features and all interact with it, or the others, you know, and that's where the complexity is, that even in the best effort, these language features always interact with each other, and that can have side effects, even such side effects, the standardization committee never thought. And there are debates, of course in the standardization committee under the conferences about us. So yeah, it's very hard, even for me, so I can imagine that those programmers who have daily work just to produce the code, and have fewer opportunities to do this. That's not easy.
Codes: Incremental SCR effort | Reactive approach
Concepts: Adoption Strategy and Timing | Execution Strategy and Effort Management
SubCategories: Execution and Operational Strategies | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: It also depends on the projects. For example, today, the projects that we work with are not up to date, they have many older versions’ frameworks, like two to three versions older. In this case, we need a detailed plan to evaluate the impacts, how much time would it take to migrate, if we have enough people to execute it, to test it, to put up an introduction. It depends a lot on the company. I have worked for three different companies here in Quebec, and the one I’m working for right now is pretty obsolete in terms of technology, so any migration process, or any new thing needs very detailed planning, containing the changes’ impact. Before this company, I was in another one that had a much better developed method. We worked on an 80/20 scale, being 80% for new features, and 20% for technical debt of the project. We always worked on the languages’ framework updates, performance improvement, error tracking, logs, this kind of thing. So, it depends a lot on the company’s working process.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate | Company/Team decision-making | Reducing technical debt
Concepts: Continuous Technical Debt Management | Decision-Making and Strategic Evaluation
SubCategories: Execution and Operational Strategies | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: Mostly, Qt already has facilities for concurrent programming. Moving from these to different ones has a far higher cost.

Quotation: No, it’s like what we’ve talked about before. There has to be some kind of benefit, And normally, the benefit consists basically of making some construction safer or (inint) [00:35:16]. There are some things that really improve performance, though not many, and it’s never a very significant change. When there’s something really significant, it’s a rare find, because the compiler people are very capable, but there are not many changes they make that you would say: wow, my performance increased magically! But there are some constructions that you can make - for example, (const express) functions [00:35:53] are always solved in compiling. This will surely impact in a positive way, there’s no way it wouldn’t.

Quotation:
K: I see. What do you think of the tools that are more ambitious in their scope, but are not fully automatic? They suggest you a change that is more complex than just rewriting...

P: We are working on such a tool. For example, one of my Ph.D. students is working on a (so-called) [01:06:32] strong typing project. What is strong typing? You have a double floating-point number type in your code, it’s a variable, and it can express a length. Another variable is another same type variable, a double can represent a time. It's better not to mess up these two types, but if these are doubles, then there is always the chance that you just change them accidentally. Instead of passing a time, you pass lengths in a function parameter, and the compiler would complain because they are just topless. Those are very typical strings. You use strings for a name. A family name. (Perhaps) a hostname. At hostname cell family names are never mixed. But if they are strings, you can mess up the use of them. If you create a separate type for the hostname, you create a separate type for the family name, and you can write a safer code, our current programming languages only use strings for that. One of my experiences in my advanced C++ lecture was that... “Okay, guys, you know, the Doxygen library, this document generator library?, that typically uses things for our establishing. Start to refactor this just to change certain types. Pick up a variable that is, I don't know a class name and create a class name type for it, and then roll on to continue this.” What's the problem here? The problem here is that, of course, if you create a hostname type then somewhere you want to add a character for that, we want to concatenate two characters now, for strings you can concatenate, but in the hostname, there are no such features. You have to implement the concatenation and then you have to increment the output function, so it's a lot of work. Also, it can possibly be that somewhere you meet, you start from here with the hostname, you start from here with the family name and there is somewhere when the army is in a function or you call the same function with both the parameters. Then how to change the parameter of that function? Sometimes the human has to decide these things. We are going to have a manual tool that makes these detections and then offers you the choice on how to decide. Can we ever have a current project for this strong typing refactoring stuff? It's not to inject new language features instead of the old, but better to use the current language features to make stronger types. I believe in these tools, because I think that in many cases automatic decisions are either impossible or just not reliable, or not worth investing in because the human can decide it much better. And to support the human. That's important. Typically, you find a word, and then you ask whether to replace it or not. Okay, that's very (basic)(01:10:57) stuff in editors, and it's working fine.
Codes: Incremental SCR effort | Tool configurability influencing SCR adoption and control
Concepts: Execution Strategy and Effort Management | Tooling Constraints and Control Mechanisms
SubCategories: Execution and Operational Strategies | Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation:
This too, little by little. Like, I've seen strategies that went wrong, this has already happened to me, now I'm going to use only this, I'm going to change everything for this, and this is going to be the standard of the project. Then it ends up breaking one thing or another, I don't expect one thing or another.

So, for new things, I try to use this new feature, or this new test method, see if it really works, sometimes we need to do it, try to come to see if that's what we think, how we understand this new feature, or this new method of making the code. So, basically, it would be like I said about the legal code, it would be doing it little by little and changing it little by little. And so, to be honest, we, at least the company, never worried about updating everything that is already done.

We try to generate value for new things, and leave new things. If at some point we need to revisit a feature, then we change to the new version of the new standard that is being used, the most recent versions of libraries or language.
Codes: Incremental SCR effort

Quotation: it’s asking for trouble. I wouldn’t do it, no. If there wasn’t a way to check everything that’s being done, I wouldn’t trust it. C++ is a multi paradigm language, that you can more or less use the way you want to, and also, has compiler extensions and improper usage that's allowed by them. It’s complicated to develop a tool that could solve everything, but tools can help, they can tell you that something should not be used in a certain way, or that you should change something. Actually, (Clang) [00:31:24] does this, Clang (Tide) [00:31:26] suggests changing a for from its present state to (head for) [00:31:31]; it suggests you stop… For example, there’s a recent suggestion. In the past, before you had (multi semantics) [00:31:46] in the language, you had to pass the string through reference. But nowadays, if you have a constructor to pass a string, and the only thing you do is to copy it to a local variable, it would be ideal to pass it through value and move it. Clang Tide already tells you that, it says: look, this string here should be passed through value and then moved on to (inint) [00:32:16]; it’s just being used to initialize another one, so move, and you can save in processing here. For this kind of thing, you have help like this, it is good. In fact, (there 's this team) [00:32:29] with a phrase, I think from Scott (Michael) [00:32:34]: programming is already difficult enough. Any help we can give to the developer is welcome. If you have a tool that can point out to you that a certain change is possible, necessary, positive, then it helps. And I’ve seen this many times, especially in compiling.
Codes: Lack of trust in programming transformation tools | Reliance on static analysis to detect SCR opportunities
Concepts: Selective and Human-Controlled Use of Tool Support | Tooling Constraints and Control Mechanisms
SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation: It gives suggestions. Some code snippets are shown like this: "the best suggestion is this, this here will depreciate, this here would be better this way". It runs on suggestions, it doesn't change everything for you, as you navigate through the code it suggests improvements. For those who program in Java and C#, there can be several versions of that framework installed on your machine, and you might wonder: will it be compatible with such and such, and when you have a very old code base in a very old version, version five Java. Suddenly you are compiling it in version eight, it will suggest to you: this section can be changed in this version. This guides you. I like it that way. I was recently using Prettier and Lint from Javascript that changes that. I'm a little bit wary of doing things that I don't know now what has done it, you know? Depending on the place and rule, I can might get confused. The tools get confused too. I use it, but with restraint and a certain distrust. I prefer to be asked if I want to change my code or not; if I want to update it to a new version. And then some read your code versioning and recommend: there is a new version, would you like to update? There's a new version. These guys are cool. You change versions, you run and break a lot of things that you have to get right manually; I kind of like it, although it takes more time to get the codebase right.
Codes: Reliance on static analysis to detect SCR opportunities | Tool configurability influencing SCR adoption and control
Concepts: Selective and Human-Controlled Use of Tool Support | Tooling Constraints and Control Mechanisms
SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation: Exactly. Clang is a static analysis tool, we are actually also developing static analysis tools, we are quite familiar with these tools. And there are, in Clang, the so-called modernizing tools which detect word constructs, which can be reflected automatically for new features. And there are two setups with these tools. One just warns you that “Hey, this is an old technique, which can be safely replaced with some new techniques.”, but also there is another version, or let's say, you give a flag, you can switch off the automatic refactoring stuff. So, that really changes the source code. Okay, so there are these kinds of refactoring tools, this Clang-Tidy library has these refactoring tools. And, actually, we are, ourselves, writing plugins for that. So, you know, this is a kind of pluggable infrastructure, there are lots of different possibilities. And this is more a framework, and you can develop different plugins to detect different possibilities and make the automatic change. So, we use that. But mostly, we just use manual rewriting. In most cases, these automatic refactoring tools can do nice, but pretty trivial changes. Major changes are not supported by tools. I don't want to say it's not possible, but on an industrial scale, I’m not aware of any C++ tools that are extremely good. This clang-tidy started to develop at Google, actually, and they are actively using it inside their codebase.
Codes: Tool configurability influencing SCR adoption and control
SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

The other would be having an easier option when in need to disable the verifications. For example, you’re working on a Java 7 project but its version is on Java 9. It doesn’t even have to be Java 7, if you’re on Java 9 using some framework, Hybernate for example, and its query was seen as a potential issue. There could be a more intuitive way to disable this check. I remember that I had to search google and explore the menu to find where I could disable the checking. So maybe a shortcut there to disable it would be a good improvement.

I use both regularly, and I think the same is true for many other KDE community members. Quotation:
W: Have you ever used a tool to evolve your code?

T: Yes. I used the migration tool from Python 2 to Python 3, but didn’t resume my work from there. I’ve used tools of code evolution in C++, a long time ago, to evolve the framework being used, Qt. When it went from version 3 to 4, there was a migration tool because there were a lot of compatibility breaks. This tool is really great, it leaves almost nothing back. These two were command line utilitarians.

W: Regarding code evolving tools: That takes a legacy code and refactor into a new one, these tools could also do this, right?

T: Yes, exactly. This Python 2 to Python 3 one takes Python 2 code and automatically refactors into analog language constructions in Python 3. The Qt 3 to Qt 4 one also did this, but in this case it wasn’t the language constructions, it was API changes, many times not trivial changes, but traits from an older API, that was a class and turned into a collaboration between many classes and vice versa, and it performed these transformations.
Codes: Using tooling to support and partially automate SCR
Concepts: Selective and Human-Controlled Use of Tool Support
SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation: When frameworks have drastic updates, they provide a migrating tool, there are also migrating guides, but preferably, an automated migration tool helps a lot. For example, from Python 2 to Python 3, they provided a migration tool, and there were changes like: In Python 2, print was a keyword, and in Python 3, a function, you could replace all print, space, anything for a print, open parenthesis, that same thing.
Codes: Using tooling to support and partially automate SCR
Concepts: Selective and Human-Controlled Use of Tool Support

Quotation: Yes, of course. Like I said, I’m taking a look, I think it’s very useful. Yes, I’ve used some scripts. I’ll amend my last answer. It was in a project that we had decided not to update, and I used a script that stated it could do this, though not 100%. In Dart 's evolution, I mentioned that I used its command line, which enables you to do this. Quotation: For example, when trying to migrate to C++ 17 in that other company, they had to disable the compiler’s warnings. Because there were incorrect things in use that were already that way in version 14, but the compiler didn’t warn us back then. Then the version 17 compiler started to not accept this kind of thing anymore, and to issue warnings. What also happens when you evolve language on C++, is that the standard starts demanding warnings. For example, there’s a situation where you use the language incorrectly and this causes issues, but they didn’t know about it. That way, as you use the language, they start to notice that this and that could be warned by the compiler. So, in the next version, they will include it in the standard, that this situation needs to be reported by the compiler. And then, a warning you didn't know starts showing up. It normally happens when there’s an incorrect use of the language, when you’re messing up.
Codes: Disabling warnings to keep systems compiling

Quotation: video.js Since Video.js components are now un-transpiled ES6 classes `extend()` needs to be able to handle them since `videojs.extend(videojs.getComponent('Component') {...})` is the most common usecase for this method. This string check is apparently the only way to detect ES6 classes. It isn't future proof and supposedly will not work on some older platforms but that is likely not a huge concern since we are 1) dropping support for most older platforms and 2) deprecating this method and encouraging the use of native classes instead.
